skill.name: aws-iam-best-practices
skill.description: Review AWS IAM policies, detect risky permissions, and plan least privilege hardening.
skill.summary: Guides AWS IAM policy review, MFA checks, access key rotation, and least privilege hardening.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples for AWS IAM auditing, not hidden runtime behavior. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network callback was found. Risk remains medium because several examples perform live IAM changes if copied into a real AWS account.

medium_findings.title: Live AWS IAM Mutation Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes AWS CLI examples that create an MFA device, create an access key, and deactivate an access key. These are legitimate IAM administration steps, but they can change a live AWS account if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and directly modify IAM resources. The surrounding context is educational hardening guidance, so this is risky administration content rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Shell Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The backticks, shell substitutions, pipes, grep, awk, and AWS CLI commands appear inside fenced Markdown examples. I found no evidence that the skill executes these commands automatically or hides command injection behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review confirms these patterns are in Markdown code blocks. They are visible user-run examples, not skill runtime code.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Benign References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP addresses are documentation example ranges, and the hardcoded URLs point to AWS IAM documentation and tools. I found no outbound request code or data exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The IP values are reserved documentation ranges and the URLs are plain resource links. There is no code that sends local data to those destinations.

low_findings.title: Keyword Blocker Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk keyword matches are ordinary IAM content, including the skill description, EC2 permissions, and EC2 role guidance. I found no C2 behavior, weak cryptography implementation, or prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is semantically tied to AWS IAM and EC2 examples. No suspicious override instructions, command-and-control endpoints, or cryptographic code were present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Potentially Destructive IAM Administration Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include commands that can create credentials and change access key status. Users should run them only in the intended AWS account after reviewing variables and impact.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The AWS CLI commands are clear and mutating. The example uses placeholder values, reducing malicious confidence but not operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution In Copyable Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The bash snippets use command substitution and loops to inspect AWS IAM resources. This is normal for audit scripts, but users should inspect them before running in privileged shells.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in documented shell examples. The intent appears legitimate, but command substitutions can still execute unexpected work if modified.

content.value_statement: AWS IAM permissions often become broad, stale, or hard to audit. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code review IAM posture and produce least privilege remediation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Identifies overly permissive IAM policies and wildcard permissions.
Guides checks for users without MFA and stale access keys.
Provides least privilege policy templates for S3 and conditional access.
Explains review steps for roles, trust policies, and unused permissions.
Generates IAM hardening checklists and audit report structures.
limitations: Does not authenticate to AWS or inspect accounts by itself.
Requires users to review commands before running them in live environments.
Does not replace AWS IAM Access Analyzer or formal security review.
Example policies need adaptation for each organization and account.

use_cases.title: Review IAM Policy Sprawl
use_cases.description: Find broad permissions, inline policies, and missing conditions before a cloud security review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Least Privilege Changes
use_cases.description: Draft safer IAM permissions for a workload that currently uses wildcard actions or resources.

use_cases.title: Build an IAM Audit Checklist
use_cases.description: Create a practical checklist for MFA, access keys, roles, trust relationships, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner IAM Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Start with a simple policy review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this IAM policy for broad permissions, missing conditions, and least privilege issues. Explain each concern in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: MFA and Access Key Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an AWS IAM audit plan for MFA coverage, old access keys, inactive users, and unused roles. Include safe read-only commands where possible.

prompt_templates.title: Least Privilege Rewrite
prompt_templates.scenario: Replace a broad policy with tighter permissions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this IAM access requirement into a least privilege policy design. Include required actions, resources, conditions, and testing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan changes for a production account.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a phased IAM hardening plan for a production AWS account. Include risk ranking, validation steps, rollback notes, and stakeholder approvals.

output_examples.input: Review a policy that allows all S3 actions on all buckets.
output_examples.output: The review flags wildcard action and resource scope, recommends limiting actions to required object operations, and adds bucket-specific resources and conditions.

output_examples.input: Create an IAM hardening report for an AWS account.
Plan access key rotation for one IAM user.
output_examples.output: The plan creates a new key, updates dependent applications, validates successful use, deactivates the old key, and deletes it after monitoring.

best_practices: Run read-only discovery commands before any command that changes IAM resources.
Test policy changes in a non-production account before rollout.
Pair generated guidance with AWS IAM Access Analyzer and CloudTrail evidence.
anti_patterns: Copying mutation commands into a privileged shell without reviewing variables.
Using wildcard actions or resources when specific permissions are known.
Treating generated IAM policies as approved without testing and peer review.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my AWS account?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example commands. Users decide what to run.

faq.question: Can it generate least privilege policies?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft policy designs and templates from stated access requirements.

faq.question: Is it safe for production IAM changes?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review every command, test changes first, and follow approval procedures.

faq.question: Does it replace AWS IAM Access Analyzer?
faq.answer: No. It complements AWS tools by helping interpret findings and plan remediation.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes copyable AWS commands that can change IAM resources if executed.

faq.question: Which assistants can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is described for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
